Press enter or click to view image in full size

4 min read
Just now

--

The manager doesn’t care about MVVM or MVI. They care about: when, how much, does it work. How to speak the language of business.

There’s a moment every developer hits when they move from junior or mid-level into something more senior. Suddenly you’re not just writing code — you’re explaining yourself to people who don’t care about your code at all.
Most developers handle this badly. Not because they’re not smart. But because nobody told them the rules changed.

The Meeting
Dev A and Dev B are back. Same characters from the last article, but they’ve both been promoted. Their direct manager is now a PM, not a tech lead.
Both get assigned the same task: build a small app. Standard stuff. They both do what the last article suggested — they set up a meeting with the manager to align before starting.
The meeting goes well. Questions get answered. Then the PM starts asking his own questions.
Dev A keeps it short. He describes his approach in plain language. Approximate timelines. General boundaries. No jargon. His answers sound dry, almost too simple. The meeting ends quickly.
Dev B goes deep. He walks through the full technical approach — architecture decisions, library choices, tradeoffs. The meeting is longer. More “complete” from a developer’s perspective. He leaves feeling like he really communicated.
Later that day, the PM goes to the product owner to discuss who should lead the project. He tries to explain what each developer said.
Dev A’s answer he can repeat almost word for word. Dev B’s answer… he summarizes as “something about architecture, seemed solid.”
Both developers get projects. But not the same ones.

Why This Happens
Developers forget one thing when talking to managers: a PM is not a technical specialist in your field.
When you say “I’ll use MVVM with a clean architecture approach, Hilt for DI, and Kotlin coroutines for async operations” — the PM hears noise. Not because he’s not smart. Because that’s not his language.
It’s not about dumbing things down. It’s about translating.
The PM’s language is: when, how much, and does it work.
That’s it. Everything else is detail he’ll never pass up the chain anyway.
Dev A understood this intuitively. Dev B learned it the hard way.

Two Paths
Here’s where it gets interesting. Both Dev A and Dev B are excellent developers. Neither made a mistake. They just revealed different strengths — and those strengths lead to very different careers.
Dev A — the one who speaks in outcomes, not implementations — will likely move toward Team Lead, then into management or product. He gets freelance clients more easily because he can run a discovery call without losing them. He closes projects faster because the conversation doesn’t get stuck in technical debate. Business people trust him quickly.
Dev B — the one who thinks in systems and can articulate every architectural decision — will likely move toward Tech Lead, then Principal Engineer or Architect. His reputation grows through the quality of what he builds. It’s a slower path to recognition, but a deeper one. Other engineers respect him. Complex problems find their way to him.
Neither path is better. Both are necessary. Every strong product team needs both people.

The Actual Skill
The mistake is thinking you have to choose who you are permanently.
You don’t. The skill is knowing which mode to switch into depending on who’s in the room.
With a PM or a founder: outcomes, timelines, risks in plain language.
With a tech lead or architect: implementation details, tradeoffs, patterns.
With a junior on your team: clear task breakdown, no assumptions.
This is what “senior” actually means in practice — not knowing more patterns, but knowing your audience.

One Thing to Remember
Before your next meeting with a manager, ask yourself one question:
Can this person repeat what I’m about to say to someone else without losing the meaning?
If the answer is no — translate first, then speak.

Where Are You Heading?
Think about which direction feels more natural to you. Not which one sounds more impressive — which one you actually want.
Both paths are long. Both require you to be good at the fundamentals. But the earlier you understand which direction pulls you, the faster you can stop optimizing for the wrong thing.
Dev A builds trust through clarity. Dev B builds trust through depth.
The best developers eventually learn to do both.

Part of the “Ship or Die” series — pragmatic development for real business. Not theory. Patterns from actual projects.
